title: Literature and Resources
text: This section provides a curated list of books and resources to enhance your understanding of Large Language Models. Each recommendation includes a brief description to help you choose the most suitable resources for you.

title: Literature and Resources
text:
Hosted LLMs
These are powerful LLMs hosted by companies, which you can access through APIs (Application Programming Interfaces). You typically pay for usage.

OpenAI (ChatGPT): The creators of ChatGPT and GPT-4, offering a range of models.
Mistral: A European-based company offering competitive models.
Google (Gemini): Google’s LLM, offering strong performance and integration with Google services.
Anthropic (Claude): Known for its its ability to handle code effectively.

title: Literature and Resources
text:
Local LLMs
These tools can be used to run open-source LLMs that you can download and run on your own machine. This gives you more privacy and control, but requires more technical expertise and computational resources.

Ollama: Free and open-source tool to run large language models locally, supports a wide range of models. Note, that the models are not as powerful as the hosted ones and that your computer needs to have a good GPU to run larger models. Smaller models with less than 8B parameters can also often be run on a CPU with enough available RAM. Great for privacy and if you don’t want to pay for the hosted models.
Hugging Face: Hosts a wide range of large language models, including models fine-tuned for specific tasks by the community. Models can also be downloaded to Ollama and run locally, if your computer is powerful enough.

title: Literature and Resources
text:
Working with data
In addition to the hosted and local LLMs, there are also tools that allow you to work with LLMs in a browser to build RAG apps or custom chatbots.

NotebookLM**: Google’s Gemini that can be fed with files, images and YouTube videos to generate text based on the content. Only works within a workspace of Google, you can’t make it available to the public (yet).
LM Studio: An application for discovering, downloading, and running LLMs locally. It supports various model architectures and offers both a Chat UI and an OpenAI-compatible local server. Features include offline document chat capabilities and easy model downloads from Hugging Face.
Open Web UI: Open Web UI is a tool to run large language models locally (in conjuction with, for example, Ollama). It is a browser-based interface that allows you to interact with the models and build RAG apps.

title: Literature and Resources
text:
AI Coding Tools

Zed: A modern, fast code editor built in Rust with built-in AI assistant support. Recommended for this course.
GitHub Copilot: AI pair programmer integrated into editors. Free for students via GitHub Student Developer Pack.
OpenCode: Open-source terminal-based AI coding assistant.
Claude Code: Anthropic’s CLI coding agent for autonomous multi-step tasks.

title: Literature and Resources
text:
AI Tools for Literature Research

Semantic Scholar: AI-powered academic search engine with related paper recommendations.
Connected Papers: Visual graph of related papers, great for discovering research clusters.
Elicit: AI research assistant that extracts key findings from papers.
Research Rabbit: Discovers related work from seed papers.
Consensus: AI search engine for research findings.

title: Literature and Resources
text:
Reference Management

Zotero: Free, open-source reference manager. Recommended for this course.
Better BibTeX for Zotero: Plugin for auto-exporting .bib files, integrates well with Quarto.

title: Literature and Resources
text:
Understanding AI

One Useful Thing: Ethan Mollick’s blog on practical AI use. Especially recommended: A Guide to Which AI to Use — explains the models, apps, and harnesses framework.
3Blue1Brown: Excellent visual explanations of how LLMs and neural networks work.

title: Literature and Resources
text:
Further Resources

Quarto: Open-source scientific publishing system. Used to create the slides and the website for this course.
Github: The largest provider for git repositories owned by Microsoft. A lot of open source projects are hosted here and you can read the code.
Daily Dose of Data Science: A website and a newsletter with lots of easy-to-digest resources to improve your skills in Data Science and Large Language Models.
Alpha Signal: A newsletter with lots of resources on the current developments of Large Language Models.

title: Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
text:
Quarto at a Glance

Quarto is an open-source scientific publishing system
Developed by Posit (formerly RStudio)
Write in Markdown, render to multiple formats:

PDF (via Typst or LaTeX)
HTML pages and websites
Presentation slides (like these!)
Word documents









Tip


Think of Quarto as a modern replacement for LaTeX that is simpler to learn, easier to use, and just as powerful for academic writing.

title: Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
text:
Why Quarto over LaTeX?


LaTeX

Steep learning curve
Complex syntax for simple tasks
Difficult to debug errors
Large installation (several GB)
Output mainly PDF


Quarto

Simple Markdown syntax
Easy to read and write
Clear error messages
Lightweight installation
PDF, HTML, Word, slides, websites









Note


Quarto produces publication-quality PDFs via Typst – no need to install a full TeX distribution!

title: Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
text:
Installation by Platform


macOS
brew install quarto
Or download the installer from quarto.org

Windows
winget install Posit.Quarto
Or download the installer from quarto.org

Linux
Download the .deb or .rpm package from quarto.org
# Debian/Ubuntu
sudo dpkg -i quarto-*.deb

title: Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
text:
PDF Output with Typst

Quarto uses Typst for PDF rendering – it is built in!
No need to install a full TeX distribution (saves several GB)
Simply set format: typst in your YAML frontmatter


---
title: "My Paper"
format:
  typst:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---








Tip


Typst is fast, modern, and produces beautiful PDFs. It compiles in milliseconds rather than seconds.

title: Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
text:
Recommended Editors

Zed – fast, modern editor with built-in Quarto support
VS Code – install the Quarto extension for syntax highlighting, preview, and more
RStudio – has native Quarto support built in








Note


Any text editor works with Quarto files since they are just plain text (.qmd files). The editors above simply provide extra convenience features like live preview and autocompletion.

title: Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
text:
YAML Frontmatter

Every Quarto document starts with a YAML header between --- markers
Controls title, author, format, and options

---
title: "My Seminar Paper"
author: "Jane Doe"
date: today
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  typst:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---







Tip


YAML uses indentation (spaces, not tabs!) to define structure. Be careful with alignment.

Your content here with [@citations] ...

title: Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
text:
How to Render

Render your document from the terminal:

quarto render paper.qmd

Or use live preview while writing:

quarto preview paper.qmd







Tip


quarto preview opens a browser window that automatically refreshes every time you save the file. This is the best way to write and check your formatting simultaneously.

title: Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
text:
Reference Management

Use Zotero (free) with the Better BibTeX extension
Better BibTeX automatically exports your library to a .bib file
Workflow: find paper, save to Zotero, cite in Quarto with [@key]








Recommended Setup



Install Zotero and the Better BibTeX plugin
Set up auto-export to references.bib in your project folder
Use the Zotero browser connector to save papers with one click
Citation keys are generated automatically (e.g., smith2023)

title: Lecture - Quarto for Academic Writing
text:
Practical Tips

Keep figures in an images/ folder for clean project structure
Use quarto preview for live preview while writing
Write one sentence per line for better version control diffs
Use <!-- comments --> to leave notes for yourself








Key Takeaway


Quarto lets you focus on content, not formatting. Write your ideas in simple Markdown, and Quarto handles the rest – citations, cross-references, numbering, and beautiful PDF output.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Welcome!

This workshop: AI Programming & Large Language Models
You have likely already used LLMs:

ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and others

By the end of this workshop, you will understand:

How LLMs work at a conceptual level
The difference between models, apps, and harnesses
Which AI coding tools to use and how to get started

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
What is an LLM?

Think of them as advanced pattern recognition systems
They have “read” massive amounts of text from the internet, books, and code
Based on those patterns, they can generate new text



LLMs really do one thing: predict the next token
Like an incredibly advanced version of autocomplete on your phone
But they cannot truly “think” or “understand” like humans









Tip


Despite their limitations, LLMs are genuinely useful for coding, writing, research, and analysis.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
The Transformer Architecture

Modern LLMs are powered by the Transformer architecture
Introduced in “Attention is All You Need” (Vaswani et al., 2017)


How it works (simplified):

Tokenization: Text is split into smaller units called tokens
Relationship mapping: The model builds a mathematical representation of how tokens relate
Attention mechanism: The model focuses on the most relevant parts of the input to generate each output token









Note


“The cat sat on the mat because it was warm.” – Attention helps the model understand that “it” refers to “the mat,” not “the cat.”

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Tokens: The Building Blocks

LLMs process text as tokens, not whole words
A token can be:

A whole word (e.g., “cat”)
A subword (e.g., “un-”, “break”, “-able”)
An individual character or punctuation mark



Why tokens?

Handles unknown words by breaking them into known subwords
More efficient than processing character by character
Trade-off: tokenization works better for some languages than others









Tip


You can explore how text is tokenized at platform.openai.com/tokenizer.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Context Window: The LLM’s Memory

The context window is the number of tokens an LLM can process at once
Think of it as the model’s short-term memory




Larger context window:

“Remembers” more of the conversation
More coherent responses
More computational cost


Smaller context window:

Faster processing
May lose track of earlier parts
Less expensive to run










Warning


Managing context windows matters when working with AI tools! Break down problems into smaller chunks.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Training Phases

Training an LLM happens in three phases:


1. Self-Supervised Learning (Pre-training)

Trained on massive text datasets without explicit labels
Learns to predict the next token – learns grammar, facts, and patterns



2. Supervised Fine-Tuning

Trained on smaller, curated datasets for specific tasks
Makes the model better at following instructions



3. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)

Human evaluators rank model outputs by quality
Model learns to produce responses that are more helpful, honest, and harmless

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
LLM Architectures (Overview)








Architecture
How it works
Best for




Encoder-Decoder
Understands input, then generates output
Translation, summarization


Encoder-Only
Focuses on understanding input
Classification, search (BERT)


Decoder-Only
Generates text token by token
Chatbots, code generation (GPT, Claude)


Mixture of Experts
Routes input to specialized sub-models
Efficient scaling (fewer params active)










Tip


Most LLMs you interact with today (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) are decoder-only models, sometimes enhanced with Mixture of Experts.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Limitations

Hallucinations: LLMs can confidently generate incorrect information
Bias: Training data contains biases that the model can reproduce
No true understanding: Pattern matching, not reasoning from first principles
Knowledge cutoff: Models only know what they were trained on



Context limits: Can lose coherence in very long conversations
Sensitivity to prompts: Small changes in wording can produce very different results









Warning


Always verify important information from LLMs. They are tools, not oracles.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
A Great Overview by 3Blue1Brown

Grant Sanderson explains LLMs visually better than most textbooks
Good starting point for the concepts we just covered
Check out his YouTube channel, 3Blue1Brown for more
Watch the video: Large Language Models explained briefly

title: Lecture - Working with AI
section: A Framework for Understanding AI Tools
text:
A Framework for Understanding AI Tools

Based on Ethan Mollick’s framework from “One Useful Thing”
Three layers to understand:



Models = the underlying AI “brains”
Apps = the interfaces you interact with
Harnesses = systems that give AI access to tools for autonomous work









Important


The same model can produce very different results depending on the harness it is placed in!

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Models: The AI Brains

The foundation model is the core intelligence
Different providers train different models:

OpenAI: GPT-4o, o3, o4-mini
Anthropic: Claude Opus, Claude Sonnet
Google: Gemini Pro, Gemini Flash
Mistral: Mistral Large, Mistral Medium
Meta: Llama (open-source)









Warning


Free/default tiers often use weaker models. Always select the frontier or advanced model when available – the difference is noticeable.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Apps: The Interfaces

Apps are how you access the models





App
URL
Provider




ChatGPT
chatgpt.com
OpenAI


Claude
claude.ai
Anthropic


Gemini
gemini.google.com
Google


Le Chat
chat.mistral.ai
Mistral






All four offer free access to capable models
Paid tiers unlock the most powerful models and higher usage limits
Mistral offers an affordable student tier with access to Mistral Vibe (not flawless, but acceptable)
The app is just the interface – the model behind it matters most

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Harnesses: The real differentiator

A harness is a system that wraps a model with tools and capabilities
Same model, different harness = completely different results


Examples of harnesses:

A chat window (Claude.ai) – conversational Q&A
A coding agent (Claude Code) – reads files, writes code, runs tests
An IDE integration (Copilot in Zed) – inline code suggestions









Tip


Pick the right harness for the task! A chat window is great for brainstorming, but a coding agent is better for building features.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:

Provider

What matters: understand the model/app/harness distinction so you can evaluate new tools as they emerge

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Why This Framework Matters

Question: Why does the same AI feel “smarter” in some tools than others?



Because the harness determines:

What context the model receives
What tools it can use (web search, file access, code execution)
How many steps it can take autonomously
How results are presented back to you










Note


When someone says “AI can’t do X,” ask: which model, in which harness? The answer often changes with the right setup.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Homebrew: Your Package Manager

Homebrew is a package manager for macOS
Installs command-line tools and applications with a single command
No more hunting for download pages or dragging .dmg files


How to install Homebrew:
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"


Then installing tools is as simple as:
brew install <tool-name>        # CLI tools

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
This workshop: AI Programming & Large Language Models
You have likely already used LLMs:

ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and others

By the end of this workshop, you will understand:

How LLMs work at a conceptual level
The difference between models, apps, and harnesses
Which AI coding tools to use and how to get started






Think of them as advanced pattern recognition systems
They have “read” massive amounts of text from the internet, books, and code
Based on those patterns, they can generate new text

. . .

LLMs really do one thing: predict the next token
Like an incredibly advanced version of autocomplete on your phone
But they cannot truly “think” or “understand” like humans

. . .






Despite their limitations, LLMs are genuinely useful for coding, writing, research, and analysis.







Modern LLMs are powered by the Transformer architecture
Introduced in “Attention is All You Need” (Vaswani et al., 2017)

. . .
How it works (simplified):


Tokenization: Text is split into smaller units called tokens
Relationship mapping: The model builds a mathematical representation of how tokens relate
Attention mechanism: The model focuses on the most relevant parts of the input to generate each output token


. . .






“The cat sat on the mat because it was warm.” – Attention helps the model understand that “it” refers to “the mat,” not “the cat.”







LLMs process text as tokens, not whole words
A token can be:

A whole word (e.g., “cat”)
A subword (e.g., “un-”, “break”, “-able”)
An individual character or punctuation mark


. . .
Why tokens?

Handles unknown words by breaking them into known subwords
More efficient than processing character by character
Trade-off: tokenization works better for some languages than others

. . .






You can explore how text is tokenized at platform.openai.com/tokenizer.







The context window is the number of tokens an LLM can process at once
Think of it as the model’s short-term memory

. . .


Larger context window:

“Remembers” more of the conversation
More coherent responses
More computational cost


Smaller context window:

Faster processing
May lose track of earlier parts
Less expensive to run



. . .






Managing context windows matters when working with AI tools! Break down problems into smaller chunks.







Training an LLM happens in three phases:

. . .
1. Self-Supervised Learning (Pre-training)

Trained on massive text datasets without explicit labels
Learns to predict the next token – learns grammar, facts, and patterns

. . .
2. Supervised Fine-Tuning

Trained on smaller, curated datasets for specific tasks
Makes the model better at following instructions

. . .
3. Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)

Human evaluators rank model outputs by quality
Model learns to produce responses that are more helpful, honest, and harmless












Architecture
How it works
Best for




Encoder-Decoder
Understands input, then generates output
Translation, summarization


Encoder-Only
Focuses on understanding input
Classification, search (BERT)


Decoder-Only
Generates text token by token
Chatbots, code generation (GPT, Claude)


Mixture of Experts
Routes input to specialized sub-models
Efficient scaling (fewer params active)



. . .






Most LLMs you interact with today (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) are decoder-only models, sometimes enhanced with Mixture of Experts.







Hallucinations: LLMs can confidently generate incorrect information
Bias: Training data contains biases that the model can reproduce
No true understanding: Pattern matching, not reasoning from first principles
Knowledge cutoff: Models only know what they were trained on

. . .

Context limits: Can lose coherence in very long conversations
Sensitivity to prompts: Small changes in wording can produce very different results

. . .






Always verify important information from LLMs. They are tools, not oracles.







Grant Sanderson explains LLMs visually better than most textbooks
Good starting point for the concepts we just covered
Check out his YouTube channel, 3Blue1Brown for more
Watch the video: Large Language Models explained briefly

section: A Framework for Understanding AI Tools
text:
A Framework for Understanding AI Tools

Based on Ethan Mollick’s framework from “One Useful Thing”
Three layers to understand:

. . .


Models = the underlying AI “brains”
Apps = the interfaces you interact with
Harnesses = systems that give AI access to tools for autonomous work


. . .






The same model can produce very different results depending on the harness it is placed in!

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Models: The AI Brains

The foundation model is the core intelligence
Different providers train different models:

OpenAI: GPT-4o, o3, o4-mini
Anthropic: Claude Opus, Claude Sonnet
Google: Gemini Pro, Gemini Flash
Mistral: Mistral Large, Mistral Medium
Meta: Llama (open-source)


. . .






Free/default tiers often use weaker models. Always select the frontier or advanced model when available – the difference is noticeable.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Apps: The Interfaces

Apps are how you access the models

. . .



App
URL
Provider




ChatGPT
chatgpt.com
OpenAI


Claude
claude.ai
Anthropic


Gemini
gemini.google.com
Google


Le Chat
chat.mistral.ai
Mistral



. . .

All four offer free access to capable models
Paid tiers unlock the most powerful models and higher usage limits
Mistral offers an affordable student tier with access to Mistral Vibe (not flawless, but acceptable)
The app is just the interface – the model behind it matters most

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Harnesses: The real differentiator

A harness is a system that wraps a model with tools and capabilities
Same model, different harness = completely different results

. . .
Examples of harnesses:


A chat window (Claude.ai) – conversational Q&A
A coding agent (Claude Code) – reads files, writes code, runs tests
An IDE integration (Copilot in Zed) – inline code suggestions


. . .






Pick the right harness for the task! A chat window is great for brainstorming, but a coding agent is better for building features.

title: Lecture - Working with AI
text:
Why This Framework Matters

Question: Why does the same AI feel “smarter” in some tools than others?

. . .

Because the harness determines:

What context the model receives
What tools it can use (web search, file access, code execution)
How many steps it can take autonomously
How results are presented back to you


. . .






When someone says “AI can’t do X,” ask: which model, in which harness? The answer often changes with the right setup.

text:
Description
Welcome to this collection of resources! This website hosts workshops and materials to help you prepare for your seminar and thesis work. The material is self-contained and covers the skills you need for your research project.


Workshops

AI Prompting — The RBTF framework for effective prompts, iteration strategies

Paper templates (German and English) are available in the course repository.


Resources

Questions
If you have any questions, please contact me under tobias.vlcek@uni-hamburg.de.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Why most people get mediocre results from AI
A simple framework to dramatically improve your prompts
The power of iteration over perfection
Common mistakes and how to avoid them

. . .






This is not about tricks or hacks. It is about clear communication — the same skill that makes you a better researcher.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Why most people get mediocre results from AI
A simple framework to dramatically improve your prompts
The power of iteration over perfection
Common mistakes and how to avoid them

. . .






This is not about tricks or hacks. It is about clear communication — the same skill that makes you a better researcher.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
The Typical Experience


What most people type:

“Help me with my literature review”


What the AI returns:

“A literature review is a comprehensive survey of scholarly sources on a specific topic. Here are some general steps: 1. Define your research question… 2. Search databases… 3. Evaluate sources…”



. . .
This is correct but useless — generic advice you could find on any study skills website.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Why Does This Happen?


The AI does not know who you are — a first-year student or a PhD candidate?
It does not know what you already have — 5 papers or 50?
It does not know what you actually need — a structure, a gap analysis, a synthesis?
It does not know how you want it — a table, a paragraph, bullet points?

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
The Core Problem






Without context, AI gives you the average answer for everyone — which helps no one.



. . .
The quality of the output is directly proportional to the quality of the input. This is true for every AI tool, whether it is ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or any other system.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Context and Iteration



Context is Everything

The more relevant detail you provide, the better the response
Think of it as briefing a new research assistant
They are brilliant but know nothing about your specific project




Iteration Beats Perfection

Your first prompt does not need to be perfect
Treat AI conversations as a dialogue, not a one-shot query
Refine, redirect, and build on each response




. . .






You would never hand a new colleague a one-sentence instruction and expect perfect results. Treat AI the same way.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Role, Background, Task, Format








Component
Question to Ask Yourself
Example




Role
Who should the AI act as?
“Act as a methodologist in social science research”


Background
What context does it need?
“I am writing my Master’s thesis on climate policy in the EU”


Task
What exactly should it do?
“Identify gaps in my literature on carbon pricing mechanisms”


Format
How should the output look?
“Present this as a table with columns: gap, relevant authors, potential research questions”

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Why RBTF Works

Role — shapes the expertise and tone of the response
Background — anchors the answer to your specific situation
Task — eliminates ambiguity about what you need
Format — ensures the output is immediately usable

. . .






You do not always need all four components. But the more you include, the better the result will be.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Context in Action: Before

“Explain machine learning”

. . .
AI Response: “Machine learning is a subset of artificial intelligence that enables systems to learn from data and improve over time without being explicitly programmed. There are three main types: supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning…”
. . .
A textbook answer. Fine if you are a complete beginner, but not tailored to anyone.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Context in Action: After

“I am a social scientist studying voting behaviour. I have no programming background, but I need to understand how machine learning could help me predict election turnout from demographic data. Explain the key concepts I would need, avoiding technical jargon, and suggest which ML approach would suit my small dataset of 2,000 observations.”

. . .
This prompt gives the AI everything it needs to provide a genuinely useful, specific response — tailored to the researcher’s discipline, skill level, and dataset.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Refining Your Results
Your first response will rarely be exactly what you need. That is normal. Use follow-up prompts to steer the conversation.
. . .







When you get…
Say this…




Too broad
“Focus specifically on [X] and go deeper”


Too technical
“Explain this for someone without a background in [field]”


Too superficial
“Provide more detail, especially regarding [aspect]”


Wrong angle
“Approach this from the perspective of [discipline/method]”


Missing something
“Also consider [factor/variable/theory]”


Too long
“Condense this to the 3 most important points”

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Iteration Example






NotePrompt 1 — Starting Point



“I need help choosing a statistical test for my research.”


. . .






NotePrompt 2 — Adding Context



“I have survey data from 200 respondents. My dependent variable is satisfaction (measured on a 5-point Likert scale) and I want to compare results across 3 age groups. I am using SPSS.”


. . .






NotePrompt 3 — Refining Further



“My data is not normally distributed according to the Shapiro-Wilk test. What non-parametric alternative should I use, and how do I run it in SPSS? Also, how should I report the results in APA format?”

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
What Changed?

Prompt 1 would get a generic overview of statistical tests
Prompt 2 gives enough context for a specific recommendation
Prompt 3 addresses a real-world complication and asks for actionable output

. . .
Each iteration builds on the previous response, turning a vague question into a concrete, usable answer. The AI retains context from the full conversation.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Before: The Lazy Prompt

“Help me with my literature review on climate change”

. . .
Result: A generic list of well-known climate change topics, basic search strategies, and textbook advice about synthesising sources. Nothing specific to your actual research.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
After: The RBTF Prompt

Role: “Act as an expert in environmental economics and systematic review methodology.”
Background: “I am a Master’s student at University of Hamburg writing my thesis on the effectiveness of carbon pricing mechanisms in the EU. I have already collected 35 papers focused on the EU Emissions Trading System from 2018–2025.”
Task: “Analyse the common themes across my collected literature and identify 3 potential research gaps that could form the basis of my thesis contribution.”
Format: “Present the themes as a structured table with columns: theme, key findings, authors, and identified gap. Then provide a brief paragraph for each gap explaining why it matters.”

text:

Without RBTF

Generic advice
Could apply to anyone
No actionable output
Requires extensive rework


With RBTF

Tailored to your project
Specific to your field
Immediately usable structure
Builds on your existing work



. . .






The RBTF prompt takes 2 minutes longer to write but saves hours of back-and-forth.

text:
Mistakes to Avoid






CautionAccepting the First Response



Always iterate. The first output is a starting point, not a final answer. Push the AI to go deeper, be more specific, or reconsider its approach.


. . .






CautionBeing Vague About Format



“Give me some ideas” produces a generic list. “Give me 5 ideas, each with a one-sentence description and a feasibility rating from 1–5” produces something you can actually work with.


. . .






CautionForgetting to Verify



AI can produce confident-sounding but incorrect information. Always verify key claims, citations, statistics, and dates against primary sources. Never cite an AI-generated reference without checking it exists.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
What We Will Cover

Why most people get mediocre results from AI
A simple framework to dramatically improve your prompts
The power of iteration over perfection
Common mistakes and how to avoid them








Tip


This is not about tricks or hacks. It is about clear communication — the same skill that makes you a better researcher.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
The Typical Experience


What most people type:

“Help me with my literature review”


What the AI returns:

“A literature review is a comprehensive survey of scholarly sources on a specific topic. Here are some general steps: 1. Define your research question… 2. Search databases… 3. Evaluate sources…”



This is correct but useless — generic advice you could find on any study skills website.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Why Does This Happen?

The AI does not know who you are — a first-year student or a PhD candidate?
It does not know what you already have — 5 papers or 50?
It does not know what you actually need — a structure, a gap analysis, a synthesis?
It does not know how you want it — a table, a paragraph, bullet points?

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
The Core Problem






Important


Without context, AI gives you the average answer for everyone — which helps no one.




The quality of the output is directly proportional to the quality of the input. This is true for every AI tool, whether it is ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or any other system.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Context and Iteration


Context is Everything

The more relevant detail you provide, the better the response
Think of it as briefing a new research assistant
They are brilliant but know nothing about your specific project


Iteration Beats Perfection

Your first prompt does not need to be perfect
Treat AI conversations as a dialogue, not a one-shot query
Refine, redirect, and build on each response









Tip


You would never hand a new colleague a one-sentence instruction and expect perfect results. Treat AI the same way.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Role, Background, Task, Format








Component
Question to Ask Yourself
Example




Role
Who should the AI act as?
“Act as a methodologist in social science research”


Background
What context does it need?
“I am writing my Master’s thesis on climate policy in the EU”


Task
What exactly should it do?
“Identify gaps in my literature on carbon pricing mechanisms”


Format
How should the output look?
“Present this as a table with columns: gap, relevant authors, potential research questions”

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Why RBTF Works

Role — shapes the expertise and tone of the response
Background — anchors the answer to your specific situation
Task — eliminates ambiguity about what you need
Format — ensures the output is immediately usable








Note


You do not always need all four components. But the more you include, the better the result will be.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Context in Action: Before

“Explain machine learning”


AI Response: “Machine learning is a subset of artificial intelligence that enables systems to learn from data and improve over time without being explicitly programmed. There are three main types: supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning…”


A textbook answer. Fine if you are a complete beginner, but not tailored to anyone.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Context in Action: After

“I am a social scientist studying voting behaviour. I have no programming background, but I need to understand how machine learning could help me predict election turnout from demographic data. Explain the key concepts I would need, avoiding technical jargon, and suggest which ML approach would suit my small dataset of 2,000 observations.”


This prompt gives the AI everything it needs to provide a genuinely useful, specific response — tailored to the researcher’s discipline, skill level, and dataset.

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Refining Your Results
Your first response will rarely be exactly what you need. That is normal. Use follow-up prompts to steer the conversation.








When you get…
Say this…




Too broad
“Focus specifically on [X] and go deeper”


Too technical
“Explain this for someone without a background in [field]”


Too superficial
“Provide more detail, especially regarding [aspect]”


Wrong angle
“Approach this from the perspective of [discipline/method]”


Missing something
“Also consider [factor/variable/theory]”


Too long
“Condense this to the 3 most important points”

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
Iteration Example






Prompt 1 — Starting Point


“I need help choosing a statistical test for my research.”










Prompt 2 — Adding Context


“I have survey data from 200 respondents. My dependent variable is satisfaction (measured on a 5-point Likert scale) and I want to compare results across 3 age groups. I am using SPSS.”











Prompt 3 — Refining Further


“My data is not normally distributed according to the Shapiro-Wilk test. What non-parametric alternative should I use, and how do I run it in SPSS? Also, how should I report the results in APA format?”

title: Lecture - AI Prompting for Researchers
text:
What Changed?

Prompt 1 would get a generic overview of statistical tests
Prompt 2 gives enough context for a specific recommendation
Prompt 3 addresses a real-world complication and asks for actionable output


Each iteration builds on the previous response, turning a vague question into a concrete, usable answer. The AI retains context from the full conversation.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
section: Use a Framework to Structure Your Question
text:
Use a Framework to Structure Your Question

The PICO framework helps you define your question clearly

. . .








Element
Meaning
Example




Population
What problem or domain?
Vehicle routing with time windows


Intervention
What approach or method?
Metaheuristic algorithms


Comparison
Compared to what?
Exact methods (branch-and-bound)


Outcome
What do you measure?
Solution quality, computation time



. . .






Not every research question fits PICO perfectly. Use it as a guide, not a strict rule.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Your Research Question Guides Everything

A well-defined question determines:

Which databases to search
Which keywords and search terms to use
Which papers are relevant and which are not
When you have covered enough ground


. . .






Write down your research question before you start searching. Revisit and refine it as you learn more about the field.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
section: Google Scholar as Your Starting Point
text:
Google Scholar as Your Starting Point

Google Scholar is the most accessible tool
Free, broad coverage, easy to use

. . .


Pros:

Free and always available
Covers most disciplines
“Cited by” feature for snowballing
Full-text search across papers


Cons:

Quality varies widely
No advanced filtering options
Includes non-peer-reviewed work
Metadata can be incomplete

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Google Scholar Tips

Use quotes for exact phrases: "vehicle routing problem"
Use the author: operator: author:taillard
Set date ranges to find recent work
Use “Cited by” to find newer papers that reference a key work
Use “Related articles” for similar papers

. . .






Create a Google Scholar profile and set up alerts for your key search terms. You will be notified when new papers match your query.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Other Important Databases
Beyond Google Scholar, use specialized databases:



Database
Strength
Access

Comprehensive, citation analysis
University login


Web of Science
High-quality journals
University login

Engineering and CS
University login

Working papers, business/economics
Free


EBSCOhost
Multi-disciplinary
University library



. . .






Access most of these through the University of Hamburg library or VPN.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Building Search Strings

Use Boolean operators to combine search terms

. . .
AND — both terms must appear:
"vehicle routing" AND "metaheuristic"
. . .
OR — either term can appear:
"genetic algorithm" OR "simulated annealing"
. . .
NOT — exclude a term:
"optimization" NOT "continuous"
. . .
Wildcards — match variations:
optimi* matches optimize, optimization, optimizing

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Putting It All Together

A complete search string for our example:

. . .
("vehicle routing" OR "VRP") AND
("metaheuristic" OR "genetic algorithm" OR
"simulated annealing") AND "optimization"
. . .

Start broad, then narrow with additional terms
Document your search strings so you can reproduce and refine them
Track how many results each search returns

. . .






Keep a search log: record the database, search string, date, and number of results for each search you run.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Snowballing

Snowballing is essential for comprehensive coverage

. . .


Forward citation tracking:

Who cited this paper?
Use Google Scholar “Cited by”
Finds newer work that builds on a key paper
Great for finding the latest developments


Backward citation tracking:

What does this paper cite?
Check the reference list
Finds foundational and seminal papers
Helps you understand the research lineage



. . .






Combine both directions. Forward snowballing finds new work; backward snowballing finds the foundations. Together, they give you the full picture.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
AI-Powered Discovery Tools

A new generation of tools uses AI to accelerate literature discovery
They complement traditional database searches

. . .



Tool
What It Does




Semantic Scholar
AI-powered academic search, finds related papers


Connected Papers
Visual graph of related papers


Elicit
AI research assistant, extracts key findings

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
More AI Tools



Tool
What It Does




Research Rabbit
Discovers related work from seed papers


Consensus
AI search engine for research findings


Perplexity
AI search with academic sources



. . .

These tools are particularly useful for:

Finding papers you would not discover through keyword search alone
Understanding how papers relate to each other
Getting a quick overview of a research field

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
section: How to Use AI Tools Effectively
text:
How to Use AI Tools Effectively

Start with a seed paper you know is relevant
Use Connected Papers to visualize the research landscape
Use Semantic Scholar to find semantically similar papers
Use Elicit to quickly extract findings across many papers

. . .






WarningImportant Caveat



AI tools are helpful for discovery, but always verify: check the actual paper, read abstracts yourself, and do not trust AI summaries blindly. AI can hallucinate references that do not exist.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Combining Traditional and AI Search

No single tool is sufficient on its own
Use a layered approach:

. . .

Start with Google Scholar and database searches
Expand with AI tools (Semantic Scholar, Connected Papers)
Snowball forwards and backwards from key papers
Verify everything you find by reading the actual papers

. . .






Think of AI tools as a way to cast a wider net, not as a replacement for careful reading and evaluation.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Why Use a Reference Manager?

You will read dozens to hundreds of papers
Without a system, you will:

Lose track of what you have read
Waste time reformatting citations
Miss papers when writing your bibliography


. . .

A reference manager helps you:

Organize papers by topic and project
Cite correctly in any format
Collaborate with others
Sync across devices

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Zotero — The Recommended Tool

Zotero is free and open-source
Works on Windows, Mac, and Linux

. . .
Key features:

Browser connector — save papers with one click
PDF storage — attach and annotate PDFs directly
Collections — organize papers into folders
Tags — add custom labels for flexible organization
Groups — share libraries for collaborative projects
Sync — access your library from any device

. . .






Always import via DOI when possible. This gives you the most accurate and complete metadata. After importing, always check that the data is correct.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Better BibTeX Plugin

Better BibTeX is essential for Quarto and LaTeX users

. . .

Auto-exports .bib files that stay in sync
Generates consistent citation keys (e.g., taillard1993)
Keeps your .bib file updated automatically when you add new papers

. . .






Set up Better BibTeX once and your bibliography file will always be up to date. No more manual .bib file editing.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
The Zotero Workflow
A streamlined workflow for your seminar or thesis:
. . .

Find a paper via Google Scholar, Scopus, or an AI tool
Save it with the Zotero browser connector or import via DOI
Organize it into a collection for your project
Auto-export a .bib file with Better BibTeX
Cite in Quarto using [@citationkey]

. . .


In your .qmd file:
Recent work by [@taillard1993]
shows that...

Rendered output:
Recent work by Taillard (1993) shows that…

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Types of Sources

Not all sources are created equal

. . .



Source Type
Reliability
Peer-Reviewed?




Journal articles
High
Yes


Conference papers
Medium–High
Usually


Preprints (arXiv)
Variable
No


Textbooks
High
Editorial review


Working papers (SSRN)
Variable
No


Blog posts, websites
Low–Variable
No



. . .






Prefer peer-reviewed journal articles and reputable conference proceedings. Use preprints and working papers with caution.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
What to Check

When evaluating a paper, consider the following quality indicators:

. . .

Journal reputation — Is it a recognized journal in the field?
Author credentials — Are the authors established researchers?
Methodology — Is the approach rigorous and well-described?
Recency — Is the paper still current, or has it been superseded?
Citations — Has it been cited by others? How many times?

. . .






For operations research specifically: check the benchmark instances used, the computational setup, and whether the results are reproducible.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Read Critically

Do not accept results at face value
Ask yourself:

. . .

Is the sample size sufficient?
Are the assumptions reasonable?
Do the conclusions follow from the results?
Can the findings be generalized to other contexts?
Are there limitations the authors do not discuss?

. . .






A good literature review is not about reading everything — it is about finding and connecting the right papers.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
The Literature Research Process
Putting it all together:
. . .

Question — Define a clear, focused research question
Search — Use databases, Boolean operators, and search strings
Snowball — Follow citations forwards and backwards
Discover — Use AI tools to find related work
Organize — Store everything in Zotero with Better BibTeX
Evaluate — Assess quality, relevance, and reliability
Write — Synthesize findings into a coherent review

. . .






Start your literature research early. It is an iterative process that improves as you learn more about your topic.

Groups — share libraries for collaborative projects
Sync — access your library from any device









Important


Always import via DOI when possible. This gives you the most accurate and complete metadata. After importing, always check that the data is correct.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Better BibTeX Plugin

Better BibTeX is essential for Quarto and LaTeX users



Auto-exports .bib files that stay in sync
Generates consistent citation keys (e.g., taillard1993)
Keeps your .bib file updated automatically when you add new papers









Tip


Set up Better BibTeX once and your bibliography file will always be up to date. No more manual .bib file editing.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
The Zotero Workflow
A streamlined workflow for your seminar or thesis:


Find a paper via Google Scholar, Scopus, or an AI tool
Save it with the Zotero browser connector or import via DOI
Organize it into a collection for your project
Auto-export a .bib file with Better BibTeX
Cite in Quarto using [@citationkey]





In your .qmd file:
Recent work by [@taillard1993]
shows that...

Rendered output:
Recent work by Taillard (1993) shows that…

Prefer peer-reviewed journal articles and reputable conference proceedings. Use preprints and working papers with caution.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
What to Check

When evaluating a paper, consider the following quality indicators:



Journal reputation — Is it a recognized journal in the field?
Author credentials — Are the authors established researchers?
Methodology — Is the approach rigorous and well-described?
Recency — Is the paper still current, or has it been superseded?
Citations — Has it been cited by others? How many times?









Note


For operations research specifically: check the benchmark instances used, the computational setup, and whether the results are reproducible.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
Read Critically

Do not accept results at face value
Ask yourself:



Is the sample size sufficient?
Are the assumptions reasonable?
Do the conclusions follow from the results?
Can the findings be generalized to other contexts?
Are there limitations the authors do not discuss?









Tip


A good literature review is not about reading everything — it is about finding and connecting the right papers.

title: Lecture - Structured Literature Research
text:
The Literature Research Process
Putting it all together:


Question — Define a clear, focused research question
Search — Use databases, Boolean operators, and search strings
Snowball — Follow citations forwards and backwards
Discover — Use AI tools to find related work
Organize — Store everything in Zotero with Better BibTeX
Evaluate — Assess quality, relevance, and reliability
Write — Synthesize findings into a coherent review









Important


Start your literature research early. It is an iterative process that improves as you learn more about your topic.
